sourceMovie.title: True Grit
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: True Grit works as a drama movie with a point of view—fans quote moments, but they also argue about what the film is actually saying about power, desire, or survival. The drama and adventure blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: News of the World
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: News of the World shares True Grit's gift for drama — the 2020 film delivers adventure without overstaying its welcome. Consider it required viewing once you've finished rewatching your first pick.

recommendations.title: Slow West
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Much of what makes True Grit compelling — the drama, the adventure — shows up in Slow West (2015) in a slightly different form. Both films understand that bittersweet storytelling works best when it trusts the audience.

recommendations.title: Jeremiah Johnson
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The drama energy of True Grit is alive in Jeremiah Johnson (1972), filtered through a different story but the same emotional register. It earns its place on this list with a tense payoff that rivals the original pick.

recommendations.title: The Cowboys
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Cowboys (1972) operates in the same drama as True Grit, but with its own distinct voice and pacing. The tense payoff will feel satisfyingly familiar without retreading the same ground.

recommendations.title: Django Unchained
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Django Unchained (2012) belongs to the same conversation as True Grit — both anchor their drama in western emotion that doesn't let go. The tense undercurrent connects them more than any plot summary would suggest.

recommendations.title: The Hateful Eight
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If True Grit's drama stayed with you, The Hateful Eight (2015) delivers the same sensation through an entirely different story. The western through-line is the real common thread between them.

recommendations.title: The Magnificent Seven
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Where True Grit excels in adventure, The Magnificent Seven (1960) doubles down on western to comparable effect. The tonal similarities run deep enough that watching both back-to-back feels intentional.

recommendations.title: Young Guns
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Young Guns (1988) is the natural next step after True Grit: same adventure DNA, different perspective. The tense streak and western elevate it well above a simple genre exercise.

recommendations.title: Hud
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Fans of True Grit who respond to its drama will find Hud (1963) immediately familiar. Both films share a western sensibility without ever feeling like carbon copies of each other.

recommendations.title: Revolutionary Road
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: There's a reason True Grit fans keep landing on Revolutionary Road (2008) — the drama is unmistakably related even when the plots diverge. Both films operate on the same emotional frequency, especially in their third acts.

faq.question: What should I watch after True Grit?
faq.answer: Try News of the World, Slow West, Jeremiah Johnson—each shares DNA with True Grit while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is True Grit?
faq.answer: True Grit (2010) is best described as Drama, Adventure, Western. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is True Grit worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 7.3/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this drama lane.
